stories.headline: Tens of Thousands Join 'No Kings' Protests Across Colorado, Including Major Capitol Rally
stories.summary:
Tens of thousands of Coloradans turned out Saturday for the third statewide round of 'No Kings' demonstrations, with the largest gathering centered at the State Capitol in Denver before spilling into surrounding streets. Organizers reported roughly 70,000 attendees in Denver alone, with traffic disruptions reported throughout the north metro area due to street closures near the Capitol and Civic Center. More than 70 protests were scheduled statewide, from Burlington to Steamboat Springs and Cortez to Fort Collins, as part of a nationwide movement that organizers said planned nearly 4,000 demonstrations across the country.

Protesters cited opposition to a range of Trump administration policies, including the use of masked federal agents in immigration enforcement, the ongoing conflict in Iran, and what demonstrators characterized as attacks on civil rights and voting rights. Signs criticized cuts to foreign aid, and several attendees dressed as suffragettes. Speakers led songs and chants and encouraged attendees to remain civically engaged.

Attendees came from across the metro area and beyond, including a 74-year-old Lochbuie resident who said it was her first large protest, and a couple from Parker who said they were dismayed by current national events. Others cited concerns about immigration enforcement and its impact on immigrant communities in Colorado.

Saturday's rally was the third in a series of statewide No Kings demonstrations, following previous events in June and October that also drew large crowds. The Colorado demonstrations mirrored activity nationally as the movement continues to grow in size and geographic reach.

stories.headline: Colorado Democratic Assembly Narrows Governor, U.S. Senate Races; Sets Primary Matchups
stories.summary:
More than 1,600 Colorado Democrats gathered Saturday at Memorial Hall in Pueblo for the party's state assembly, setting up a series of head-to-head primary contests ahead of the June 30 election. Attorney General Phil Weiser won more than 90% of delegate support in the gubernatorial race and will face U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet, who petitioned onto the ballot rather than participating in the assembly. In the U.S. Senate race, state Sen. Julie Gonzales of Denver captured the top-line ballot position, setting up a primary against Sen. John Hickenlooper, who also bypassed the assembly in favor of petitioning.

In the treasurer's race, state Sen. Jeff Bridges was the only candidate to clear the required 30% delegate threshold, while competitors state Rep. Brianna Titone and John Mikos fell just short. The secretary of state race between state Sen. Jessie Danielson and Jefferson County Clerk Amanda Gonzalez will move forward with both candidates after Gonzalez won the top-line spot with 63% of the vote. The attorney general race saw Secretary of State Jena Griswold narrowly edge attorney David Seligman for the top-line position, 42% to 41%.

Neither Hickenlooper nor Bennet appeared at the Pueblo event, a notable absence that reflects a strategic calculation by both incumbents to appeal directly to broader primary voters rather than the more activist assembly delegates. Republicans are scheduled to hold their own state assembly in Pueblo in two weeks.

The results set the stage for a competitive primary season across Colorado's top statewide offices, with the June 30 primary now less than three months away. Recent Colorado history suggests assembly outcomes do not always predict primary winners, as Bennet in 2010 and Hickenlooper in 2020 both lost assembly votes before winning their respective primaries.

stories.headline: Diana DeGette Barely Survives District Assembly Challenge From Melat Kiros, Setting Up Contested Primary
stories.summary:
Fifteen-term U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette narrowly cleared the 30% delegate threshold needed to qualify for Colorado's June 30 primary at the party's 1st Congressional District assembly Friday, receiving just over 33% of delegate votes compared to 67% for challenger Melat Kiros, a 28-year-old first-time candidate. The result is the closest call DeGette, 68, has faced since she was first elected in 1996 to represent the district covering Denver and portions of Arapahoe County. A third candidate, University of Colorado Regent Wanda James, is attempting to petition her way onto the ballot; the secretary of state's office has until late April to verify her signatures.

Kiros, a former New York securities attorney whose parents immigrated from Ethiopia, launched her campaign last summer and has drawn endorsements from the national Justice Democrats organization and Denver Democratic Socialists of America. She has attacked DeGette for accepting corporate PAC contributions and for what she describes as insufficient opposition to the Trump administration. The assembly result closely mirrors a tally from two weeks earlier at the Denver County Democratic assembly, where Kiros also decisively outpaced DeGette among party activists.

DeGette countered that her experience in Washington gives Denver Democrats an advantage, arguing that the district needs seasoned leadership to fight the Trump administration and position the party for a House majority. She has won previous primaries by wide margins and in 2024 defeated her Republican opponent by 55 percentage points in one of the largest margins for a contested congressional race in state history.

The 1st Congressional District is among the most reliably Democratic U.S. House seats in the country, having not sent a Republican to Congress since 1970. Republicans have yet to field a candidate for the seat this cycle, meaning the June primary is likely to determine the district's next representative.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Advances Bill Allowing Large Affordable Housing Projects in Single-Family Zones
stories.summary:
A bill that would allow construction of three-story affordable housing apartment projects in any residential zoning district in Colorado — including areas currently zoned for single-family or two-family housing — has passed both chambers of the state legislature and is awaiting the governor's signature. Under HB 26-1001, such projects could be undertaken by any government entity, school district, or nonprofit, including churches, and would require only administrative approval from city housing officials with no public input process for neighboring property owners.

The Colorado Municipal League and the City of Colorado Springs officially opposed the measure, which supporters say is intended to smooth the path for affordable senior housing projects similar to one being developed by Silver Key, an elderly services organization in Colorado Springs. Critics argue the bill strips local governments of zoning authority and removes community input from land-use decisions that could significantly alter the character of established neighborhoods.

A separate measure, HB 26-1308, also advanced through the legislature this session and eases the process for splitting residential lots, allowing an administrative approval process with no public comment. The bill removes a prior requirement that any new dwelling on a split lot be maintained as a rental, allowing the new parcel and home to be sold as a separate property.

The housing bills are part of a broader multi-year trend in the Colorado legislature to assert state authority over local zoning in an effort to increase housing density and affordability, particularly near major urban centers. Opponents argue the cumulative effect of these measures is to override decisions that residents and local governments have traditionally made for themselves.

stories.headline: Avalanche Host Jets in Home-and-Home Finale, Looking to Extend Win Streak Atop NHL Standings
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche returned home Sunday to host the Winnipeg Jets in the back half of a home-and-home series, looking to extend their win streak after a 3-2 victory in Winnipeg on Thursday. The Avs enter the game as the NHL's top team, holding a nine-point lead over the Dallas Stars in the Central Division with a game in hand, and sit first overall in the league standings. A win would further tighten their grip on home-ice advantage throughout the Stanley Cup Playoffs.

Thursday's victory in Winnipeg featured two third-period goals from Nathan MacKinnon, who deflected in the eventual game-winner off a pass from Artturi Lehkonen. Lehkonen, who had missed 11 games with an injury, returned in that contest and factored directly into the decisive goal. The Jets, sitting at 30-30-12, are battling for positioning in the Western Conference wild-card race.

Coach Jared Bednar said after Thursday's game that the team gave up a couple of goals on turnovers along the defensive-zone walls and identified that as an area to clean up in the rematch. The Avs completed a four-game road trip sweep heading into the game, having also won in Chicago, Washington, and Pittsburgh.

The Central Division standings remain fluid behind Colorado. Dallas has dropped multiple recent games, allowing the Minnesota Wild to close to within three points of second place in the division, with Dallas holding a game in hand over Minnesota. The Avalanche's comfortable lead means they are largely playing for seeding and momentum as the regular season approaches its final weeks.

stories.headline: DU Pioneers Advance to Face Western Michigan With Frozen Four Berth on the Line
stories.summary:
The University of Denver men's hockey team cruised past Cornell 5-0 in the first round of the NCAA tournament Friday at Blue Arena in Loveland, setting up a second-round matchup Saturday against Western Michigan with a trip to the Frozen Four at stake. The lopsided victory demonstrated Denver's offensive depth and continued the program's recent tradition of deep tournament runs. Several Colorado Avalanche players with ties to both programs were noted to be following the tournament closely.

The game has added intrigue given the teams' history: Cornell knocked Denver out of the 2023 NCAA tournament, and Friday's rematch drew attention from Avalanche players who attended both programs. DU's Sean Behrens won two national championships with the Pioneers in 2022 and 2024, while Cornell alumni on the Avalanche roster include Sam Malinski, Hank Kempf, and Matt Stienburg.

The regional is being held at Blue Arena in Loveland, giving the Denver program a significant home-region advantage. A victory over Western Michigan would send the Pioneers back to the Frozen Four, cementing DU's status as one of college hockey's premier programs in recent years.

The NCAA hockey tournament is running concurrently with the NBA and NHL regular season stretch runs, making for a particularly busy stretch on the Colorado sports calendar. Denver's performance in Loveland has drawn attention from the broader hockey community in the state.

stories.headline: Nuggets' Cam Johnson Delivers Clutch Corner Threes to Lift Denver Past Utah, Keep Playoff Positioning Intact
stories.summary:
Cam Johnson hit two pivotal corner three-pointers in the final minutes of Friday's game against the Utah Jazz, including the go-ahead basket that preserved a Denver Nuggets victory and kept the team a game ahead in the tight Western Conference standings. Johnson, who had missed seven of nine three-point attempts earlier in the game, connected on back-to-back corner threes in the closing minutes after Nikola Jokic set him up with precise passes out of the post. The win extended Denver's recent strong run, having won six of their last seven games.

Johnson, who was the salutatorian of his high school class and earned a master's degree from North Carolina, spoke after the game about the psychology of shooting and the importance of continuing to fire regardless of earlier misses. Coach David Adelman praised Johnson's shooting ability but noted his one persistent critique is when Johnson declines open looks. Johnson is shooting 39.5% from three-point range for his career.

The Nuggets have climbed to fourth in the Western Conference after a troubling mid-season stretch that saw them fall as low as sixth. With five of their remaining seven regular-season games at home, including upcoming matchups against Golden State, Portland, Memphis, Oklahoma City, and San Antonio, Denver has a favorable path to solidify or improve their seeding heading into the playoffs.

Nikola Jokic and Jamal Murray continue to anchor the offense, and the team is considered fully healthy for the first time this season. Denver is 9-7 against other projected Western Conference playoff teams this season, though they remain 0-3 against the Oklahoma City Thunder with one regular-season matchup remaining.

stories.headline: Denver Office Vacancy Rate Approaches 40% Downtown Even as Five Developers Add New Space
stories.summary:
Metro Denver continues to grapple with a substantial surplus of commercial office space, with downtown Denver's vacancy rate approaching 40% as empty skyscrapers sell at steep discounts to their original values. The elevated vacancy rate reflects the lasting impact of remote and hybrid work trends on demand for traditional office space in the urban core, a pattern shared by many major American cities but particularly acute in Denver.

Despite the oversupply, five developers are moving forward with plans to add nearly 600,000 square feet of new office space in the Denver area this year and into early next year, according to a report from CommercialCafe, a division of real estate research firm Yardi. The continued construction activity runs counter to the broader market signal of weak demand and high vacancy, raising questions about long-term absorption of the new supply.

The disconnect between surging vacancy rates and ongoing development reflects a mix of factors, including projects that were planned or financed before the remote-work shift became permanent, as well as developer confidence in specific submarkets or building classes that are outperforming the overall market. Class A and newer properties have generally fared better than older office stock in the downtown core.

The office market's struggles have broader implications for Denver's downtown economy, affecting everything from retail foot traffic to city tax revenues. City and regional officials have explored office-to-residential conversion programs and other strategies to reactivate underutilized buildings, but the scale of the vacancy challenge remains one of the most significant issues facing downtown Denver's long-term recovery.

stories.headline: Colorado Newspaper Industry Awaits State Action as New Mexico Passes Tax Credits for Local Journalism
stories.summary:
New Mexico Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham last week signed legislation offering tax credits to businesses that employ local journalists and to companies that print newspapers, making New Mexico only the fourth state — alongside California, New York, and Illinois — to take legislative action to address the declining number of local news outlets. The move has reignited a conversation in Colorado about whether the state should enact similar measures to support a local journalism industry that has shed more than 50 newspapers in the past decade.

According to the Colorado News Mapper project, 52 local newspapers in Colorado have closed over the last 10 years, leaving at least three counties with no local news outlets. The trend mirrors a national crisis in which the loss of local reporting has left communities with less information about government, public safety, and civic affairs — what advocates describe as the growth of 'news deserts.'

The Denver Gazette's editor in chief Vince Bzdek drew a historical parallel to the Postal Service Act of 1792, which provided deeply discounted mailing rates for newspapers as a deliberate policy choice by the nation's founders to ensure the flow of civic information. He argued that public support for journalism has historical precedent and is not a departure from democratic norms.

Colorado lawmakers have not advanced comparable legislation this session. Supporters of a state-level response argue that the market alone will not stabilize local news ecosystems and that government intervention — through tax incentives rather than direct subsidies — represents a proportionate and legally sound approach to preserving civic infrastructure.

stories.headline: CBI Issues Missing Indigenous Person Alert for 15-Year-Old Lakewood Girl Last Seen in February
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation has issued a Missing Indigenous Person Alert for a 15-year-old girl from Lakewood who was last seen in February, officials announced. The alert is intended to mobilize public awareness and assist law enforcement in locating the teenager, whose disappearance spans more than a month. Specific details about her last known location and circumstances of her disappearance were included in the alert to help the public provide tips.

The Missing Indigenous Person Alert is a relatively recent designation in Colorado, modeled on the Amber Alert system and designed to address a disproportionate rate of missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls. Advocates have long called attention to the underreporting and inadequate law enforcement response historically associated with cases involving Indigenous individuals.

Anyone with information about the girl's whereabouts is urged to contact the Colorado Bureau of Investigation or local law enforcement. The CBI maintains a tip line and online reporting tools for missing persons cases.

The case highlights ongoing concerns about the safety and visibility of Indigenous youth in Colorado, particularly in the Denver metro area. Advocacy organizations have pressed state and local governments to dedicate more resources to investigating and publicizing missing Indigenous persons cases.

stories.headline: Archbishop James Golka Formally Installed as Leader of Denver's Catholic Archdiocese
stories.summary:
Archbishop James Golka was formally installed Saturday as the leader of the Archdiocese of Denver in a ceremony held at CoBank Arena, marking the official transition of leadership for one of the largest Catholic dioceses in the Rocky Mountain region. The installation ceremony is a formal ecclesiastical rite that formally seats a new archbishop in his diocese and is typically attended by clergy, civic leaders, and members of the Catholic faithful from across the archdiocese.

Golka, who previously served as bishop of the Diocese of Colorado Springs, was appointed to lead the Denver Archdiocese following the retirement of his predecessor. The Archdiocese of Denver encompasses a large geographic area across northern and central Colorado and serves hundreds of thousands of Catholics through its parishes, schools, and social services organizations.

The choice of CoBank Arena — a large venue in the Denver suburb of Loveland — reflects the scale of the event and the size of the Catholic community expected to attend. Installation ceremonies of this significance draw participants from across the archdiocese and sometimes from neighboring dioceses and the Vatican.

Golka's installation marks a new chapter for the archdiocese as it navigates ongoing issues facing the Catholic Church nationally, including questions around clergy accountability, demographic shifts in the Catholic population, and the church's role in public life. His leadership of the Colorado Springs diocese will serve as a reference point as he takes on the larger and more complex Denver archdiocese.

stories.headline: New 600-Acre Wildfire Burns Near Walsenburg in Southern Colorado With No Containment
stories.summary:
A new wildfire spanning approximately 600 acres erupted near Walsenburg in Huerfano County in southern Colorado, with firefighting crews reporting no containment as of the latest update. The fire adds to a difficult spring wildfire season for Colorado, which has already seen multiple significant blazes across the state amid dry and warm conditions. Huerfano County is a largely rural area in southern Colorado near the Culebra Range of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

Details on the cause of the fire and any structures threatened were not immediately available in early reports. Firefighting resources have been stretched statewide this season as drought conditions and above-normal temperatures have elevated wildfire risk across much of Colorado. The Denver metro area recorded its warmest March temperatures on record this month, contributing to tinder-dry conditions across the Front Range and southern plains.

The Walsenburg-area fire comes on the heels of a series of other significant wildfire events in Colorado this spring, including a blaze on Fort Carson that closed Colorado 115 for more than a week. Smoke from regional fires has periodically blanketed the Denver metro area, generating large volumes of 911 calls from concerned residents.

State and local emergency management officials have urged residents across southern Colorado to remain alert to changing fire conditions and to follow evacuation orders promptly if issued. Drought forecasts suggest elevated wildfire risk is likely to persist across much of Colorado through at least early summer.

weather.notable: About 24 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: In terms of instant relief, canceling plans is like heroin.

topStory.headline: Pentagon Draws Up Plans for Weeks of Ground Operations in Iran as Troops Flood Into Middle East
topStory.summary:
The Pentagon has developed contingency plans for potential ground operations inside Iran that could last weeks, The Washington Post reported Saturday, as thousands of U.S. troops and Marines continue arriving in the Middle East. The plans stop short of a full-scale invasion and would instead involve targeted raids carried out by Special Operations forces and conventional infantry units. Officials speaking anonymously said the missions could expose personnel to Iranian drones and missiles, ground fire, and improvised explosive devices.

President Donald Trump has publicly denied any intention to send troops into Iran, stating on March 20, 'I'm not putting troops anywhere.' White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed the planning is underway but said it does not indicate a decision has been made, noting it is the Pentagon's duty to provide the commander in chief with maximum options. Secretary of State Marco Rubio separately told reporters the conflict 'is not going to be a prolonged conflict' and that U.S. objectives can be achieved without ground forces.

Discussions among U.S. officials have included the possible seizure of Kharg Island, Iran's primary oil export hub in the Persian Gulf, as well as coastal raids near the Strait of Hormuz aimed at neutralizing threats to commercial and military shipping. Iran has already bolstered its defenses of Kharg Island in anticipation of a potential operation, according to CNN. Estimated timelines from officials range from 'weeks, not months' to 'a couple of months.'

The planning comes amid an ongoing U.S.-Israeli military campaign against Iran that began February 28 and has killed more than 1,300 people, including then-Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, according to TRT World. Iran has retaliated with drone and missile strikes targeting Israel, Jordan, Iraq, and Gulf states hosting U.S. military assets. Thirteen American service members have been killed and more than 300 wounded in Iranian retaliatory attacks across at least seven countries over the past month.

Public opposition to a ground campaign remains substantial. An AP-NORC poll found 62 percent of U.S. adults strongly oppose deploying ground troops in Iran, and a Reuters/Ipsos survey found 55 percent do not support the idea. Nevertheless, 65 percent of Americans surveyed by Reuters/Ipsos said they expect Trump to ultimately launch a large-scale ground operation.
